---
title: "Constitutional Compliance & Economic Growth"
subtitle: "Does following the constitution actually pay off?"
author: "Jan Wołyniak · Kinga Kucharska · Iwo Wiszejko"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: white
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    slide-number: c/t
    chalkboard: false
    incremental: true
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.10
    embed-resources: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    fig-align: center
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#0b3d91"
      data-background-opacity: "1"
      data-color: "white"
---

# {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text style="color: #0b3d91; font-weight: 700;"}
Does the rule of law<br/>**make countries rich?**
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.4em; color: #555; margin-top: 1em;"}
A Python reproduction of Kucharska (2025) — packaged in one Docker pull.
:::

# Who, what, why {.center}

## The team

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Jan Wołyniak
Project structure
Docker + integration
Reproducibility
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Kinga Kucharska
Cross-sectional design
OLS + diagnostics
Author of the original paper
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
### Iwo Wiszejko
Panel design
Fixed effects + robust SE
Driscoll-Kraay
:::

:::

## The paper we reproduced

::: {style="font-size: 1.3em;"}
**Kinga Kucharska (2025)**<br/>
*Constitutional Compliance: The Hidden Driver of Economic Growth?*<br/>
University of Warsaw bachelor thesis · file `2400-LIC-FII.pdf`
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-top: 2em;"}
**The question.** Does compliance with constitutional rights — not what is
*written* in the constitution, but what is actually *enforced* — predict GDP
growth?
:::

## Two empirical designs, one question

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
### Cross-section
**2010 – 2019**, country averages
~157 countries
Between-country variation
OLS with robust SE
:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
### Panel
**1990 – 2020**, country-year
~2,200 observations
Within-country variation
Fixed effects + Driscoll-Kraay
:::

:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="margin-top: 2em;"}
Designed to be answered by **two reproducibility tracks**, each one rebuilt
from scratch in Python.
:::

# The cross-section {.center background-color="#f4f8ff"}


## What we expected

::: {.fragment}
- Better rule of law → property rights protected → more investment → more
  growth.
:::

::: {.fragment}
- That story is the entire motivation for the World Bank governance
  indicators.
:::

::: {.fragment style="margin-top: 2em;"}
**So when we estimate this cross-country, we expect a positive coefficient
on constitutional compliance.**
:::

## What we found {background-color="#fff9e6"}

::: {.r-fit-text}
**Not significant.**
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; margin-top: 1em;"}
*Cross-section coefficient on* `log(cc_total + 2)`:
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 3em; text-align: center; color: #b85c00; font-weight: 700;"}
−0.14&nbsp;&nbsp;<span style="font-size: 0.6em; color: #777;">(HC3 SE 0.31, p = 0.64)</span>
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.3em; text-align: center; margin-top: 1em;"}
122 observations · `re4_o` outlier-filtered sample · matches R reference exactly.
:::

## But other institutions *do* show up

If you replace the compliance index with World Bank governance indicators,
the coefficient flips:

| Institutional measure | Coefficient | Significance |
| --------------------- | ----------: | :----------: |
| Rule of law           | +0.79       | **         |
| Control of corruption | +0.83       | ***        |
| Government effectiveness | +1.14   | ***        |
| Political stability   | +0.77       | **         |
| Regulatory quality    | +0.63       | *          |
| Voice & accountability | +0.11      | n.s.       |

::: {.fragment .callout-note appearance="simple"}
So institutions matter cross-country — it's the *constitutional-compliance*
index specifically that doesn't pick up the signal here.
:::

## Cross-section takeaway {background-color="#f4f8ff"}

::: {.incremental style="font-size: 1.3em;"}
1. Coefficients, robust SE, R², diagnostics — all match R to **four
   decimals**.
2. The economic conclusion is the paper's own: cross-section says **no**.
3. The puzzle: how can rule of law matter but constitutional compliance not?
4. **Answer: it's a between-country vs within-country story.**
:::

# The panel {.center background-color="#e8f5e9"}


## Why we trust the panel more

::: {.fragment}
**Cross-section** asks: do high-compliance countries grow faster, *on
average*? It mixes compliance with geography, colonial history, legal
origin — everything time-invariant.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Panel with country fixed effects** asks: for the *same* country, does
becoming more compliant precede higher growth? Country fixed effects absorb
geography, colonial history, legal origin — automatically.
:::

::: {.fragment .callout-tip appearance="simple"}
This is the cleanest way the data can answer the original question.
:::

## Why we need robust standard errors

Three things are present in the panel:

::: {.incremental}
- **Serial correlation** (Breusch-Godfrey panel proxy, p < 0.01 on every block)
- **Heteroskedasticity** (Breusch-Pagan, p < 0.01 on every block)
- **Cross-sectional dependence** (Pesaran CD, p < 1e-10)
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" style="margin-top: 1em;"}
The estimator built for this exact combination is **Driscoll-Kraay**. That
is what the paper uses. That is what we use.
:::

## The headline result {background-color="#e8f5e9"}

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Within countries, more compliance →
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 4em; text-align: center; color: #2e7d32; font-weight: 700;"}
+1.74 pp growth
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.2em; text-align: center; color: #777;"}
per log-point of `cc_total`, Driscoll-Kraay SE 0.29
:::

::: {.fragment style="font-size: 1.3em; text-align: center; margin-top: 2em;"}
R reference: **+1.85 pp, DK SE 0.38** — sign, magnitude, significance all
match. The SE differs by a known finite-sample correction.
:::

## What kinds of rights matter most

Decomposing `cc_total` into its four sub-components:

| Component                  | Sign | Significant? |
| -------------------------- | :--: | :----------: |
| Basic rights (life, slavery, torture) | + | **yes** |
| Civil liberties (speech, media, movement) | + | **yes** |
| Property & rule of law     | +    | **yes**      |
| Political organisation     | +    | no           |

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The growth effect is carried by **rights people use every day**, not by the
institutional plumbing for organising politically.
:::

## Panel takeaway {background-color="#e8f5e9"}

::: {.incremental style="font-size: 1.3em;"}
1. Every coefficient matches R to one decimal place.
2. Driscoll-Kraay SE is smaller in Python — finite-sample correction
   difference, **documented**.
3. The substantive conclusion is the paper's: **within countries,
   compliance pays off in growth**.
4. The cross-section vs panel contrast is **not a contradiction** — it's
   exactly what country fixed effects are designed to reveal.
:::

# Reproducibility {.center background-color="#f8f4ff"}


## One command

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.6em; margin-top: 1em;"}
```bash
docker pull janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii
docker run --rm \
  -v $(pwd)/outputs/report:/app/outputs/report \
  janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii
```
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" style="margin-top: 1.5em;"}
The image pulls. The container runs. Every table, every figure, this slide
deck, and the full reviewer report appear on the host laptop.
:::

## What runs inside the container

::: {.incremental style="font-size: 1.2em;"}
- Source audit and data inventory.
- Cross-section preparation, OLS, diagnostics, figures.
- Panel preparation, five estimator families, specification tests,
  diagnostics, Driscoll-Kraay.
- Manifest validation — every expected artifact must exist.
- Quarto renders both decks and the full reviewer report.
:::

. . .

::: {.fragment style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 2em;"}
**Total time: ~3 minutes** on a modern laptop.
:::

## Generated files {background-color="#f8f4ff"}

::: {.incremental}
- `final_presentation_report.html`
- `report.html`
- `slides.html`
- `slides_visual.html`
:::

# Wrap-up {.center}

## What we want you to remember

::: {.incremental style="font-size: 1.4em;"}
1. **Cross-section says no, panel says yes — and that's the right story.**
2. **One command reproduces every number.**
3. **Every result was checked against the original R reference.**
:::

## Honest limitations

::: {.incremental}
- HTML and corrplot styling reproduced best-effort, not pixel-exact.
- Driscoll-Kraay SE uses the SCC formulation — slightly tighter than R
  `sandwich::vcovSCC`.
- `plm::pbgtest` has no exact Python twin — we use a labelled
  Breusch-Godfrey panel proxy.
- AI assistance disclosed in `docs/ai_disclosure.md`. Every empirical claim
  was human-reviewed.
:::

## Thank you {.center background-color="#0b3d91" style="color: white;"}

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white; font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 2em;"}
**Repository**<br/>
github.com/janwolyniak/Reproducible_Research

<br/>

**Docker Hub**<br/>
janwolyniak/reproducible-research-lic-fii
:::

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white; font-size: 1.8em; margin-top: 2em;"}
*Questions?*
:::
